description: Plugin code review system. When an MCP client encounters a plugin with permission 'off', the server guides the agent through a review flow: inspect the adapter IIFE source code, let the AI review it with comprehensive security guidance, get human confirmation, then mark it as reviewed and set the desired permission. Review tokens enforce that plugin_inspect must be called before plugin_mark_reviewed. Reviewed state is tracked per-version — plugin updates reset permission to 'off' and require re-review. The side panel shows an unreviewed icon on plugin cards and a confirmation dialog when users try to enable unreviewed plugins manually.

userStories.title: Add reviewedVersion to PluginPermissionConfig and automatic version reset
userStories.description: As a developer, I want plugin review state tracked per-version so that plugin updates force re-review
acceptanceCriteria: PluginPermissionConfig in shared/src/index.ts gains optional 'reviewedVersion?: string' field
OpentabsConfig in config.ts persists reviewedVersion per plugin

During plugin registry load (reload.ts), for each plugin: if state.pluginPermissions[name].reviewedVersion exists and differs from the plugin's installed version, override permission to 'off' and clear reviewedVersion
The permission reset is persisted to config.json immediately via savePluginPermissions
When a plugin has no reviewedVersion in config (fresh install), its permission remains at its configured value (default 'off')
Unit tests: version mismatch resets permission to 'off', version match preserves permission, absent reviewedVersion preserves permission, reset is persisted

If they differ, set permission to 'off', clear reviewedVersion, and persist. The plugin version comes from the registry entry (plugin.version from package.json).

userStories.title: Implement review token generation and validation in server state
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a review token system so that plugin_mark_reviewed can only be called after plugin_inspect
acceptanceCriteria: ServerState gains a 'reviewTokens: Map<string, ReviewToken>' field
ReviewToken type: { plugin: string; version: string; createdAt: number; used: boolean }
generateReviewToken(state, plugin, version) creates a crypto.randomUUID() token, stores it in the map, and returns the token string
validateReviewToken(state, token, plugin, version) returns true only if: token exists in map, token.plugin matches, token.version matches, token is not used, token is not expired (10 minute TTL)
consumeReviewToken(state, token) marks the token as used so it cannot be reused
Expired tokens are lazily cleaned up on each generateReviewToken call (remove tokens older than 10 minutes)

Unit tests: generate returns unique tokens, validate succeeds for fresh token, validate fails for wrong plugin, validate fails for wrong version, validate fails for expired token, validate fails for already-used token, consume marks token as used
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
userStories.notes:
Files to modify:
- platform/mcp-server/src/state.ts — Add ReviewToken type and reviewTokens Map to ServerState. Add generateReviewToken, validateReviewToken, consumeReviewToken functions. Update createState to initialize reviewTokens as new Map().
- platform/mcp-server/src/state.test.ts — Add comprehensive tests for the token lifecycle.

The token is a plain crypto.randomUUID() string — no JWT, no signing. It's a server-side session token. The map is in-memory only (lost on restart, which is fine — restart invalidates all pending reviews).

userStories.title: Implement plugin_inspect MCP tool with review guidance
userStories.description: As an MCP client, I want a plugin_inspect tool so that I can retrieve and review plugin adapter source code before enabling it
acceptanceCriteria: New MCP tool 'plugin_inspect' is registered with the server
Tool accepts { plugin: string } as input
Tool returns: plugin name, version, author (from package.json), npm package name, adapter IIFE source code (full text), line count, byte size, and a review token
The review token is generated via generateReviewToken and included in the response
If the plugin does not exist in the registry, returns an error
If the plugin has no adapter file, returns an error explaining this
The response includes comprehensive review guidance text instructing the AI what to look for (see notes for full guidance)
The tool is NOT shown in the side panel tool list (it is a platform built-in tool)
The tool description in MCP is: 'Retrieve plugin adapter source code for security review. Call this before enabling an unreviewed plugin.'
Unit tests: returns source code for valid plugin, returns error for unknown plugin, generates review token, review guidance text is included
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
userStories.notes:
Files to modify:
- platform/mcp-server/src/mcp-setup.ts — Register plugin_inspect as a new tool. Add it to the platform tools section alongside plugin_list_tabs, extension_get_state, etc. The tool handler reads the adapter IIFE file from the plugin's resolved path on disk.
- platform/mcp-server/src/mcp-tool-dispatch.ts — Add handlePluginInspect function. It reads the adapter IIFE file content using fs.readFile, gathers metadata from the plugin registry entry (name, version, author, npmPackage), computes line count and byte size, generates a review token, and assembles the response.

To read the adapter IIFE: the plugin registry entry has a resolved path. The adapter file is at `<pluginPath>/dist/adapter.iife.js` (ADAPTER_FILENAME from shared constants). Read it as UTF-8.

The review guidance text to include in the response (as a 'reviewGuidance' field in the text content):

```
Review this plugin adapter code and report your findings to the user. Be thorough and honest.

## What to check

### 1. Code readability
- Is the code minified, obfuscated, or bundled into unreadable single-line blocks?
- The official build tool (opentabs-plugin build) produces unminified, readable JavaScript with original variable names and formatting.
- Minified or obfuscated code is a significant red flag — it means the author bypassed the standard build process or is intentionally hiding the implementation.
- Look for intentionally misleading variable names or code structures designed to obscure intent.

### 2. Data exfiltration
- Does the code make network requests to ANY external domains? Look for: fetch(), XMLHttpRequest, navigator.sendBeacon(), WebSocket connections, new Image() with src (pixel tracking), dynamic script/iframe injection that loads external URLs.
- Plugin adapters should ONLY interact with the page DOM of the target web application. Any outbound requests to third-party domains are suspicious.
- Check for encoded/obfuscated URLs (base64, string concatenation to build URLs, hex-encoded strings).
- Look for data being serialized and sent anywhere — JSON.stringify of DOM content, form data collection, or page scraping beyond what the plugin's stated tools require.

### 3. Credential and sensitive data access
- Does the code access: document.cookie, localStorage, sessionStorage, indexedDB, Web Crypto API keys, password input fields, authentication tokens, session identifiers, or OAuth tokens?
- Does it read or modify HTTP-only cookie attributes?
- Does it access the Clipboard API (navigator.clipboard)?
- Does it intercept or modify form submissions?
- These APIs are not needed for normal plugin tool operation.

### 4. Code execution vectors
- Does the code use: eval(), new Function(), setTimeout/setInterval with string arguments, dynamic script injection (createElement('script')), document.write(), innerHTML with unsanitized content, import() with dynamic URLs?
- These can be used to execute arbitrary code at runtime, potentially loading malicious payloads after the initial review.

### 5. DOM manipulation beyond stated purpose
- Does the code's DOM interaction match the plugin's stated purpose?
- A Slack plugin should read/write Slack-specific DOM elements (message inputs, channel lists, etc.) — not access unrelated page content.
- Look for broad DOM queries (document.querySelectorAll('*'), document.body.innerHTML) that scrape entire page content rather than targeted element access.
- Does it attach global event listeners (keydown, input, submit, beforeunload) that could monitor user activity beyond the plugin's scope?

### 6. Destructive actions
- Even if the code only interacts with the target web service, does it perform any potentially destructive actions?
- Look for: mass deletion patterns (delete all, remove all, clear all), bulk modification of user data, account settings changes, permission escalation, automated posting/messaging without clear user intent, subscription or billing modifications.
- Check if the tool implementations match their declared names and descriptions — a tool named 'list_messages' should not be deleting or modifying messages.

### 7. Persistence and stealth
- Does the code set up any persistence mechanisms? Look for: Service Worker registration, browser extension message passing to unknown targets, periodic timers that run after the tool completes, mutation observers or intersection observers that trigger background behavior, web worker creation.
- Does the code attempt to hide its activity by suppressing console output, catching and silencing errors, or modifying browser DevTools behavior?

### 8. Scope escalation
- Does the code attempt to access cross-origin resources or iframes?
- Does it modify the page's Content Security Policy?
- Does it interact with browser APIs beyond DOM manipulation (geolocation, camera, microphone, notifications, Bluetooth)?
- Does it attempt to interact with other browser tabs or windows?

## How to report
Provide a clear, honest summary to the user:
1. Overall assessment: safe / suspicious / dangerous
2. What the code does (in plain language)
3. Any concerns or red flags found
4. Your recommendation: enable or keep disabled

Do not downplay concerns. If anything is suspicious, say so clearly.
```

The tool should NOT be visible in the side panel. To achieve this, check how other platform tools (plugin_list_tabs, extension_get_state) are already filtered out of the side panel tool list in the extension sync payload. Add plugin_inspect to the same exclusion list.

userStories.title: Implement plugin_mark_reviewed MCP tool with token validation
userStories.description: As an MCP client, I want a plugin_mark_reviewed tool so that I can mark a plugin as reviewed and set its permission after the human confirms
acceptanceCriteria: New MCP tool 'plugin_mark_reviewed' is registered with the server
Tool accepts { plugin: string, version: string, reviewToken: string, permission: 'ask' | 'auto' } — 'off' is not allowed as it would be pointless
Tool validates the review token via validateReviewToken — returns error if invalid, expired, wrong plugin/version, or already used
On valid token: consumes the token, sets the plugin permission in state.pluginPermissions, sets reviewedVersion to the provided version, persists to config
Calls callbacks.onToolConfigChanged() to emit tools/list_changed notification (description prefix updates)
Sends plugins.changed notification to the extension so the side panel updates
Returns a confirmation message: 'Plugin <name> v<version> has been reviewed and permission set to <permission>.'
The response text also includes a process note: 'Note: This tool should only be called after the user has explicitly confirmed they want to enable the plugin following your code review.'
Tool is NOT shown in the side panel tool list

Tool description in MCP: 'Mark a plugin as reviewed and set its permission. Requires a valid review token from plugin_inspect. Only call this after the user has reviewed and approved your security assessment.'

Unit tests: succeeds with valid token, fails with invalid token, fails with expired token, fails with used token, fails with wrong plugin, fails with wrong version, permission and reviewedVersion are persisted, tools/list_changed is emitted
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
userStories.notes:
Files to modify:
- platform/mcp-server/src/mcp-setup.ts — Register plugin_mark_reviewed as a new tool alongside plugin_inspect.
- platform/mcp-server/src/mcp-tool-dispatch.ts — Add handlePluginMarkReviewed function. Validates inputs, validates token, consumes token, updates state, persists config, sends notifications.

The tool explicitly does NOT accept permission='off' — the only valid values are 'ask' and 'auto'. If someone passes 'off', return an error.

The response includes a process note reminding the AI that this should only follow human confirmation. This is a soft guardrail on top of the hard guardrail (the token). Even if a model ignores the note, the token still enforces that plugin_inspect was called first.

Add plugin_mark_reviewed to the same side panel exclusion list as plugin_inspect.

userStories.title: Update error response for 'off' plugins to guide agent through review flow
userStories.description: As an MCP client, I want clear instructions when a tool call fails due to 'off' permission so that I know how to guide the user through the review process
acceptanceCriteria: When the agent calls a tool on a plugin with permission 'off', the error response includes specific instructions about the review flow

The error message differs based on whether the plugin has been previously reviewed: if reviewedVersion exists but doesn't match (version update), the message says 'Plugin X has been updated to vY.Z and needs re-review'; if no reviewedVersion exists, the message says 'Plugin X has not been reviewed yet'

The error message includes: instruction to ask the user if they want a code review, mention of plugin_inspect tool, mention that the user can alternatively enable it from the side panel directly, the plugin name and version
The error message does NOT include the review token — the agent must call plugin_inspect to get one

For browser tools (plugin='browser'), the review flow is skipped — browser tools are platform built-ins and do not have adapter IIFE files. Their 'off' error message simply says the tool is disabled and to enable it in the side panel
Unit tests: error message for unreviewed plugin, error message for updated plugin, browser tool error message has no review flow
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
userStories.notes:
Files to modify:
- platform/mcp-server/src/mcp-tool-dispatch.ts — In the permission='off' branch of both handleBrowserToolCall and handlePluginToolCall, construct the appropriate error message. For plugin tools, check state.pluginPermissions[pluginName].reviewedVersion to determine if this is a fresh plugin or a version update.

Error message template for plugin tools:
```
Plugin "<name>" (v<version>) has not been reviewed yet.

You can help the user by:
1. Ask the user if they would like you to review the plugin's code for security before enabling it.
2. If they agree, call the plugin_inspect tool to retrieve the adapter source code.
3. Review the code and share your findings with the user.
4. If the user approves, call plugin_mark_reviewed to enable the plugin.

Alternatively, the user can enable this plugin directly from the OpenTabs side panel.
```

For version updates:
```
Plugin "<name>" has been updated from v<old> to v<new> and needs re-review.
... (same steps)
```

For browser tools (no review flow):
```
Tool "<name>" is currently disabled. Ask the user to enable it in the OpenTabs side panel.
```

userStories.title: Add unreviewed icon to plugin cards in the side panel
userStories.description: As a user, I want to see which plugins have not been reviewed so that I can identify plugins that need attention
acceptanceCriteria: Plugin cards in the side panel show an unreviewed indicator icon when the plugin's current version has not been reviewed (reviewedVersion is absent or doesn't match installed version)
The icon is a small shield with a question mark (ShieldQuestion from lucide-react) or equivalent, displayed next to the plugin name in the card header
The icon has a tooltip: 'This plugin version has not been reviewed'
The icon uses a muted/warning color (text-muted-foreground or text-amber) to draw subtle attention without being alarming
When the plugin IS reviewed (reviewedVersion matches installed version), no icon is shown (clean state, no clutter)
Browser tools card never shows the unreviewed icon (browser tools are platform built-ins)
The reviewed state is included in the sync.full payload from server to extension: each plugin in the payload includes 'reviewed: boolean' (server compares reviewedVersion against installed version)
PluginCard.stories.tsx includes stories for reviewed and unreviewed states

userStories.title: Add unreviewed plugin confirmation dialog to side panel
userStories.description: As a user, I want a confirmation dialog when I try to enable an unreviewed plugin so that I am aware of the review option before enabling
acceptanceCriteria: When the user changes an unreviewed plugin's permission from 'off' to 'ask' or 'auto' in the side panel, a retro Dialog modal appears before the change is applied
Dialog header: 'Unreviewed Plugin' (yellow bg-primary, consistent with other dialogs)

Dialog body shows: plugin name and version, text explaining this plugin version has not been reviewed, suggestion that the user can ask their AI agent to review the adapter code, note that they can proceed without review
Dialog footer has two buttons: 'Cancel' (outline) and 'Enable Anyway' (primary)
Clicking 'Cancel' closes the dialog and does not change the permission
Clicking 'Enable Anyway' closes the dialog, sets reviewedVersion to the current version (marking it as user-accepted-without-review), sets the permission to the user's chosen value, and persists both
The dialog does NOT appear when changing between 'ask' and 'auto' on an already-reviewed plugin
The dialog does NOT appear when changing permission to 'off' (disabling is always fine)
The dialog does NOT appear for browser tools
Dialog has a Storybook story
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
userStories.notes:
Files to modify:
- platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/components/PluginCard.tsx — Intercept permission change from 'off' to 'ask'/'auto'. If plugin is not reviewed, show the dialog instead of immediately changing. Store pending permission change in local state.
- Create new component UnreviewedPluginDialog.tsx (or inline in PluginCard). Uses retro Dialog primitive.

Dialog structure:
```tsx
<Dialog open={showUnreviewedDialog}>
  <Dialog.Content onInteractOutside={e => e.preventDefault()}>
    <Dialog.Header>Unreviewed Plugin</Dialog.Header>
    <Dialog.Body>
      <Text className='font-mono text-sm'>{plugin.name} v{plugin.version}</Text>
      <Text className='text-sm mt-2'>
        This plugin version has not been reviewed. You can ask your AI 
        agent to review the adapter code by saying "review the {plugin.name} 
        plugin" in your chat.
      </Text>
      <Text className='text-xs text-muted-foreground mt-2'>
        You can also enable it now without review.
      </Text>
    </Dialog.Body>
    <Dialog.Footer>
      <Button variant='outline' onClick={cancel}>Cancel</Button>
      <Button onClick={enableAnyway}>Enable Anyway</Button>
    </Dialog.Footer>
  </Dialog.Content>
</Dialog>
```

When 'Enable Anyway' is clicked: call a new bridge method that sets both the permission AND the reviewedVersion. This requires a server-side handler that accepts both fields in one call — extend config.setToolPermission or config.setPluginPermission to optionally accept reviewedVersion.

- platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/bridge.ts — Add setPluginPermissionWithReview method or add optional reviewedVersion param to existing setPluginPermission.
- platform/mcp-server/src/extension-handlers.ts — Update handleConfigSetPluginPermission to accept optional reviewedVersion param.
- platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/components/UnreviewedPluginDialog.stories.tsx — Storybook story.

userStories.title: Hide review tools from side panel and ensure they bypass permission checks
userStories.description: As a developer, I want plugin_inspect and plugin_mark_reviewed to be invisible in the side panel and exempt from the permission system
acceptanceCriteria: plugin_inspect and plugin_mark_reviewed do not appear in the side panel tool list
They are not included in the browser tools section or any plugin section of the sync.full payload
They are always executable regardless of permission settings (they are platform infrastructure, not user-facing tools)
They do not appear in the MCP tools/list with any [Disabled] or [Requires approval] prefix
They are registered as platform tools in the same category as plugin_list_tabs, extension_get_state, etc.

Files to modify:
- platform/mcp-server/src/mcp-setup.ts — Register plugin_inspect and plugin_mark_reviewed in the platform tools section. They should bypass getToolPermission checks entirely (same as plugin_list_tabs). They should not be included in the list sent to the extension for side panel display.
- platform/mcp-server/src/extension-handlers.ts — Ensure these tools are excluded from the browserTools array in the sync.full payload. Check how extension_get_state, extension_get_logs, plugin_list_tabs are already excluded and follow the same pattern.

Platform tools are a distinct category from browser tools and plugin tools. They are always available, always executable, and never shown in the side panel permission UI.

userStories.title: E2E tests for the plugin review flow
userStories.description: As a developer, I want comprehensive E2E tests for the review system so that the full flow is verified end-to-end
acceptanceCriteria: E2E test: agent calls a tool on an 'off' plugin → receives error with review instructions → calls plugin_inspect → receives source code and review token → calls plugin_mark_reviewed with valid token → plugin permission is set → agent retries the original tool → succeeds
E2E test: agent calls plugin_mark_reviewed with invalid token → receives error
E2E test: agent calls plugin_mark_reviewed without calling plugin_inspect first → receives error (no token)
E2E test: plugin version changes → permission resets to 'off' → agent encounters review flow again
E2E test: side panel shows unreviewed icon for unreviewed plugins
E2E test: side panel shows confirmation dialog when enabling unreviewed plugin, 'Enable Anyway' sets reviewedVersion and permission, 'Cancel' does nothing
E2E test: reviewed plugin (reviewedVersion matches) does not show unreviewed icon, side panel permission change works without dialog
E2E test: plugin_inspect and plugin_mark_reviewed do not appear in side panel
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
npm run test:e2e passes
userStories.notes:
Files to modify:
- e2e/plugin-review.e2e.ts — New E2E test file. Create comprehensive tests for the full review flow.
- e2e/fixtures.ts — Add fixture helpers for creating test plugins with known adapter IIFE content. The test plugin's adapter should be a simple, readable IIFE that the test can verify is returned by plugin_inspect.

For the version change test: the fixture needs to simulate a plugin version change. This could be done by modifying the test plugin's package.json version between test steps, then triggering a reload.

For the side panel tests: use Playwright to interact with the side panel. Check for the ShieldQuestion icon, click the permission selector, verify the dialog appears, click buttons, verify state changes.

For the token tests: call plugin_mark_reviewed with a fabricated token string and verify the error. Call it without calling plugin_inspect first (no valid token exists) and verify the error.
